name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يُجَـٰدِلُ فِى ٱللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ وَيَتَّبِعُ كُلَّ شَيْطَـٰنٍ مَّرِيدٍ (٣)
```

```english
Among the people are those who dispute about Allah without any knowledge, and follow every froward devil, (3)
```

```arabic
كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِ أَنَّهُۥ مَن تَوَلَّاهُ فَأَنَّهُۥ يُضِلُّهُۥ وَيَهْدِيهِ إِلَىٰ عَذَابِ ٱلسَّعِيرِ (٤)
```

```english
about whom it has been decreed that he will mislead those who take him for an ally, and conduct them toward the punishment of the Blaze. (4)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ إِن كُنتُمْ فِى رَيْبٍ مِّنَ ٱلْبَعْثِ فَإِنَّا خَلَقْنَـٰكُم مِّن تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ مِن نُّطْفَةٍ ثُمَّ مِنْ عَلَقَةٍ ثُمَّ مِن مُّضْغَةٍ مُّخَلَّقَةٍ وَغَيْرِ مُخَلَّقَةٍ لِّنُبَيِّنَ لَكُمْ ۚ وَنُقِرُّ فِى ٱلْأَرْحَامِ مَا نَشَآءُ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ثُمَّ نُخْرِجُكُمْ طِفْلًا ثُمَّ لِتَبْلُغُوٓا۟ أَشُدَّكُمْ ۖ وَمِنكُم مَّن يُتَوَفَّىٰ وَمِنكُم مَّن يُرَدُّ إِلَىٰٓ أَرْذَلِ ٱلْعُمُرِ لِكَيْلَا يَعْلَمَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ عِلْمٍ شَيْـًٔا ۚ وَتَرَى ٱلْأَرْضَ هَامِدَةً فَإِذَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْهَا ٱلْمَآءَ ٱهْتَزَّتْ وَرَبَتْ وَأَنۢبَتَتْ مِن كُلِّ زَوْجٍۭ بَهِيجٍ (٥)
```

```english
O people! If you are in doubt about the resurrection, [consider that] We created you from dust, then from a drop of [seminal] fluid, then from a clinging mass, then from a fleshy tissue, partly formed and partly unformed, so that We may manifest [Our power] to you. We lodge in the wombs whatever [fetus] We wish for a specified term, then We bring you forth as infants, then [We rear you] so that you may come of age. [Then] there are some of you who are taken away, and there are some of you who are relegated to the nethermost age, such that he knows nothing after [having possessed] some knowledge. And you see the earth torpid, yet when We send down water upon it, it stirs and swells, and grows every delightful kind [of plant]. (5)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَأَنَّهُۥ يُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ وَأَنَّهُۥ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٦)
```

```english
All that is because Allah is the Reality and it is He who revives the dead, and He has power over all things, (6)
```

```arabic
وَأَنَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ ءَاتِيَةٌ لَّا رَيْبَ فِيهَا وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَبْعَثُ مَن فِى ٱلْقُبُورِ (٧)
```

```english
and because the Hour is bound to come, there is no doubt in it, and Allah will resurrect those who are in the graves. (7)
```

```arabic
وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يُجَـٰدِلُ فِى ٱللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ وَلَا هُدًى وَلَا كِتَـٰبٍ مُّنِيرٍ (٨)
```

```english
Among the people are those who dispute concerning Allah without any knowledge or guidance, or an enlightening Book, (8)
```

```arabic
ثَانِىَ عِطْفِهِۦ لِيُضِلَّ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ لَهُۥ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا خِزْىٌ ۖ وَنُذِيقُهُۥ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ عَذَابَ ٱلْحَرِيقِ (٩)
```

```english
turning aside disdainfully to lead [others] astray from the way of Allah. For such there is disgrace in this world, and on the Day of Resurrection We will make him taste the punishment of the burning: (9)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ يَدَاكَ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَيْسَ بِظَلَّـٰمٍ لِّلْعَبِيدِ (١٠)
```

```english
‘That is because of what your hands have sent ahead, and because Allah is not tyrannical to the servants.’ (10)
```

```arabic
وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَعْبُدُ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ حَرْفٍ ۖ فَإِنْ أَصَابَهُۥ خَيْرٌ ٱطْمَأَنَّ بِهِۦ ۖ وَإِنْ أَصَابَتْهُ فِتْنَةٌ ٱنقَلَبَ عَلَىٰ وَجْهِهِۦ خَسِرَ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْخُسْرَانُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (١١)
```

```english
And among the people are those who worship Allah on the [very] fringe: if good fortune befalls him, he is content with it; but if an ordeal visits him he makes a turnabout, to become a loser in the world and the Hereafter. That is manifest loss. (11)
```

```arabic
يَدْعُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَضُرُّهُۥ وَمَا لَا يَنفَعُهُۥ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلضَّلَـٰلُ ٱلْبَعِيدُ (١٢)
```

```english
He invokes besides Allah that which can bring him neither benefit nor harm. That is extreme error. (12)
```

```arabic
يَدْعُوا۟ لَمَن ضَرُّهُۥٓ أَقْرَبُ مِن نَّفْعِهِۦ ۚ لَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَوْلَىٰ وَلَبِئْسَ ٱلْعَشِيرُ (١٣)
```

```english
He invokes someone whose harm is surely likelier than his benefit. An evil ally indeed, and an evil companion! (13)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُدْخِلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَفْعَلُ مَا يُرِيدُ (١٤)
```

```english
Allah will indeed admit those who have faith and do righteous deeds into gardens with streams running in them. Indeed Allah does whatever He desires. (14)
```

```arabic
مَن كَانَ يَظُنُّ أَن لَّن يَنصُرَهُ ٱللَّهُ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ فَلْيَمْدُدْ بِسَبَبٍ إِلَى ٱلسَّمَآءِ ثُمَّ لْيَقْطَعْ فَلْيَنظُرْ هَلْ يُذْهِبَنَّ كَيْدُهُۥ مَا يَغِيظُ (١٥)
```

```english
Whoever thinks that Allah will not help him in this world and the Hereafter, let him stretch a rope to the ceiling and hang himself, and see if his artifice would remove his rage. (15)
```

```arabic
وَكَذَٰلِكَ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ ءَايَـٰتٍۭ بَيِّنَـٰتٍ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَهْدِى مَن يُرِيدُ (١٦)
```

```english
Thus have We sent it down as manifest signs, and indeed Allah guides whomever He desires. (16)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses mention various categories of people: those who persist in falsehood and dispute the truth, and those who are wavering in it. They describe their condition, clarify their straying, and depict their evil end. They also mention the believers, noting that they are guided in this world and blessed in the Hereafter.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يُجادِلُ فِي اللهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ وَيَتَّبِعُ كُلَّ شَيْطانٍ مَرِيدٍ» — *“Among the people is he who disputes concerning Allah without knowledge, and follows every rebellious Satan”* (22:3). The **rebellious (المريد)** is the wicked one; it is also said to mean one who is stripped of all good and devoted to corruption. Disputing concerning Allah without knowledge refers to speaking about His attributes and acts based on ignorance while persisting in it.

His saying: «وَيَتَّبِعُ كُلَّ شَيْطانٍ مَرِيدٍ» — *“and follows every rebellious Satan”* — is an exposition of his path in belief and action, following the exposition of his path in speech. It is as if it were said: he speaks concerning Allah without knowledge, persists in his ignorance, believes in every falsehood, and acts upon it. Since it is Satan who leads man to falsehood, and man only inclines toward it through his seduction, he follows Satan in everything he believes and does. Thus, the "following of Satan" is placed in the verse in the position of belief and action to indicate the manner of his conduct, and to clarify in the subsequent verse that he is straying from the path of Paradise and traveling toward the punishment of the Blaze.

The Exalted said: «وَيَتَّبِعُ كُلَّ شَيْطانٍ» — *“and follows every Satan”* — and did not say: "and follows the rebellious Satan," meaning Iblis. This is to indicate his involvement in various arts and types of misguidance, for the doors of falsehood are diverse, and at every door is a Satan from the likes of Iblis and his progeny. There are also Satans among humans who call toward misguidance, and their erring followers imitate and follow them, even if every enticement and whisper ultimately traces back to Iblis, may Allah curse him.

Furthermore, the phrase «وَيَتَّبِعُ كُلَّ شَيْطانٍ» — *“and follows every Satan”* — is an allusion to his lack of restraint in following falsehood; he does not stop at any limit because his capacity for truth has been nullified and his heart has been sealed. It carries the same meaning as His saying: «وَإِنْ يَرَوْا سَبِيلَ الرُّشْدِ لا يَتَّخِذُوهُ سَبِيلاً وَإِنْ يَرَوْا سَبِيلَ الغَيِّ يَتَّخِذُوهُ سَبِيلاً» — *“If they see the way of rectitude, they do not take it as [their] way, and if they see the way of error, they take it as [their] way”* (7:146).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِ أَنَّهُ مَنْ تَوَلَّاهُ فَأَنَّهُ يُضِلُّهُ وَيَهْدِيهِ إِلى عَذابِ السَّعِيرِ» — *“It has been written against him that whoever takes him for a friend, he will surely lead him astray and guide him to the punishment of the Blaze”* (22:4). **Taking as a friend (التولي)** means taking him as a guardian to be followed. His saying: «فَأَنَّهُ يُضِلُّهُ» — *“he will surely lead him astray”* — etc., is a subject whose predicate is omitted. The meaning is: he follows every rebellious Satan whose attribute is that it has been written against him that whoever takes him as a guardian and follows him, then his misguiding him and guiding him to the punishment of the Blaze is a fixed and inevitable matter.

What is meant by it being "written against him" is the Divine Decree (القضاء الإلهي) regarding him: first, by misguiding his followers, and second, by entering them into the Fire. These two decrees are what is referred to in His saying: «إِنَّ عِبادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغاوِينَ وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمَوْعِدُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ» — *“Indeed, as for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, except such of the perverse as follow you. And indeed hell is the promised place for them all”* (15:42-43). The explanation of this has already been provided in the twelfth volume of this book.

From what has preceded, the weakness of the view that the meaning is "whoever takes Satan as a friend, then Allah misguides him" becomes apparent. There is no evidence in the speech of the Exalted for such a claimed "writing"; rather, what is mentioned in His speech is the decree of giving Iblis authority over those who take him as a friend and follow him, as mentioned before.

Furthermore, that interpretation would necessitate a discrepancy in the pronouns, making the pronoun in «فَأَنَّهُ» — *“he will surely”* — refer to something not previously mentioned without any justification.

Even weaker is the view of those who say the meaning is: "it was written against this person who disputes concerning Allah without knowledge that whoever takes him as a friend, he will misguide him"—returning the pronouns to the relative pronoun in "he who disputes." This is clearly untenable.

It appears from the verse that the decree against Iblis is a decree against his tribe, his progeny, and his helpers; their misguiding and their "guiding" to the punishment of the Blaze—in short, their action—is his action. The subtle irony in combining "misguides him" and "guides him" in the verse is not hidden.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ فِي رَيْبٍ مِنَ الْبَعْثِ فَإِنَّا خَلَقْناكُمْ مِنْ تُرابٍ» — *“O mankind! If you are in doubt about the Resurrection, then [consider that] We created you from dust...”* (22:5) until His saying: *“...anything.”* The Resurrection (البعث) — *the raising of the dead and the return to Allah, the Glorified* — is the intended meaning here, which is evident. The **clot (العلقة)** is a piece of congealed blood. The **lump (المضغة)** is a piece of flesh that looks as if it has been chewed. The **formed (المخلقة)**, as has been said, is that which is complete in its creation, while the **unformed (غير المخلقة)** is that which is incomplete. This corresponds to the fashioning of the embryo that is concomitant with the breathing of the spirit (نفخ الروح) into it; thus, the view that "creation" (تخليق) here means "fashioning" (تصوير) is consistent with this.

His saying: «لِنُبَيِّنَ لَكُمْ» — *“so that We may clarify for you”* — the context suggests the meaning is: so that We may clarify for you that the Resurrection is possible and remove your doubt. For witnessing the transition from dead dust to a drop (نطفة), then to a clot, then to a lump, then to a living human being, leaves no room for doubt regarding the possibility of the dead being clothed with life. For this reason, His saying: «لِنُبَيِّنَ لَكُمْ» — *“so that We may clarify for you”* — was placed at this point and not delayed until the end of the verse.

His saying: «وَنُقِرُّ فِي الْأَرْحامِ ما نَشاءُ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى» — *“and We lodge in the wombs whatever We wish until a specified term”* — means We keep in the wombs whichever embryos We wish and do not cause them to miscarry until the completion of the term of pregnancy. «ثُمَّ نُخْرِجُكُمْ طِفْلاً» — *“then We bring you forth as infants.”* It is said in *al-Majma‘*: "Meaning, We bring you out from the bellies of your mothers while you are infants (أطفال)." An infant (الطفل) is the small one among humans. It is mentioned in the singular even though the plural is intended because it functions as a verbal noun (مصدر), like their saying: "a just man" (رجل عدل) and "just men" (رجال عدل). It is also said that it means: "then We bring each one of you forth as an infant." End quote. Reaching **maturity (الأشد)** refers to the state when the limbs and faculties become strong.

His saying: «وَمِنْكُمْ مَنْ يُتَوَفَّى وَمِنْكُمْ مَنْ يُرَدُّ إِلى أَرْذَلِ الْعُمُرِ» — *“and among you is he who is taken [unto Him], and among you is he who is relegated to the most abject age”* — the contrast between the two sentences indicates that the first is qualified by what distinguishes it from the second. The implied meaning is: "and among you is he who is taken before he is relegated to the most abject age." The **most abject age (أرذل العمر)** refers to the most contemptible and lowly part of it, which corresponds to the state of senility; it is the most abject part of life when compared to what preceded it.

His saying: «لِكَيْلا يَعْلَمَ مِنْ بَعْدِ عِلْمٍ شَيْئاً» — *“so that he does not know anything after [having possessed] knowledge”* — means anything significant that those who live value and upon which they build their lives. The "so that" (اللام) denotes the ultimate end (الغاية); i.e., his condition ends in the weakness of faculties and senses such that nothing remains of the knowledge—which is the most precious harvest of life—that can be relied upon for living.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَتَرَى الْأَرْضَ هامِدَةً فَإِذا أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْهَا الْماءَ اهْتَزَّتْ وَرَبَتْ وَأَنْبَتَتْ مِنْ كُلِّ زَوْجٍ بَهِيجٍ» — *“And you see the earth barren, but when We send down water upon it, it stirs and swells, and grows every delightful kind [of plant]”* (22:5). Al-Raghib said: "It is said: the fire **subsided (همدت)** when it went out. From this comes 'barren land' (أرض هامدة) in which there is no vegetation, and 'withered vegetation' (نبات هامد) which is dry. The Exalted said: «وَتَرَى الْأَرْضَ هامِدَةً»." End quote. Close to this is the interpretation of it as "lifeless land."

He also said: **Stirring (الهز)** is vigorous movement. It is said: "I shook the spear and it quivered (اهتز)." Vegetation "stirs" (اهتز) when it moves due to its freshness. He also said: **Swells (ربا)** means it increased and rose. The Exalted said: «فَإِذا أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْهَا الْماءَ اهْتَزَّتْ وَرَبَتْ» — meaning it increased with the growth of that which is nurtured. End quote (summarized).

His saying: «وَأَنْبَتَتْ مِنْ كُلِّ زَوْجٍ بَهِيجٍ» — *“and grows every delightful kind”* — means the earth grows every category of plant characterized by **delight (البهجة)**, which is beauty of color and the appearance of joy in it. Or, **pair (الزوج)** refers to that which is opposite to the single, for His speech establishes "pairing" for plants just as it establishes life for them, and contemporary empirical sciences have confirmed this.

The summary is that the earth, in its sprouting and growing of vegetation, has a status similar to that of the womb in its vital growth of dust that becomes a drop, then a clot, then a lump, until it becomes a living human being.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللهَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ وَأَنَّهُ يُحْيِ الْمَوْتى وَأَنَّهُ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ» — *“That is because Allah is the Reality, and because He revives the dead, and because He has power over all things”* (22:6). "That" (ذلك) refers to what was mentioned in the previous verse regarding the creation of man and plants and the management of their affairs in their inception and continuation—realities of creation and management in which there is no doubt.

What the context provides is that "the Reality" (الحق) refers to Reality itself—meaning it is not an adjective standing in place of an omitted noun as the predicate. Rather, He, the Exalted, is Reality itself, who realizes every true thing and conducts the true system within things. His being the Reality (الحق) through whom every true thing is realized is the cause for these true existents and the true systems running within them; they all disclose that He, the Exalted, is the Reality.

His saying: «وَأَنَّهُ يُحْيِ الْمَوْتى» — *“and because He revives the dead”* — is coordinated with what preceded it. That is, what was mentioned in the previous verse—the transformation of dead dust through various states into a living human, and likewise the transformation of dead earth through the descent of water into living plants—and the continuation of this matter is because Allah revives the dead and continues to do so.

His saying: «وَأَنَّهُ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ» — *“and because He has power over all things”* — is coordinated with the preceding phrase like its predecessor. The meaning is that what We have mentioned is because Allah has power over all things. This is because the origination of man and plants and the management of their affairs in inception and continuation are linked to the existence and the system governing existence in the cosmos. Just as their origination and management cannot be completed except with power over them, likewise, power over them cannot be completed except with power over all things. Thus, their creation and management are due to the universality of His power; or if you wish, say: that discloses the universality of His power.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَأَنَّ السَّاعَةَ آتِيَةٌ لا رَيْبَ فِيها وَأَنَّ اللهَ يَبْعَثُ مَنْ فِي الْقُبُورِ» — *“and that the Hour is bound to come, there is no doubt in it, and that Allah will resurrect those who are in the graves”* (22:7). These two sentences are coordinated with the "that" (أن) in His saying: «ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللهَ» — *“That is because Allah...”*

As for the reason for singling out these five results mentioned in the two verses for mention—even though the exposition of the preceding verse yields other important results in the chapters of monotheism (التوحيد), such as His Lordship, the negation of partners in worship, His being All-Knowing, the Bestower of Blessings, the Generous, and so on:

What the context provides—and the context is one of establishing the Resurrection—and by comparing these verses to other verses establishing the Resurrection, is that the verse aims to prove the Resurrection by way of proving that He, the Exalted, is the Absolute Reality (الحق على الإطلاق). For Pure Reality (الحق المحض) does not produce anything but a true act, not a vain one. If there were no other realm in which man lives according to his felicity or wretchedness, and creation were limited to origination then annihilation, then origination then annihilation, and so on, it would be a vain play (لعب باطل). Thus, His being the Reality who does nothing but the truth necessitates the realm of Resurrection with a clear necessity; for this worldly life is cut short by death, so there must inevitably be another, everlasting life after it.

Thus, the verse—meaning His saying: «فَإِنَّا خَلَقْناكُمْ مِنْ تُرابٍ» until His saying: «ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللهَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ» — follows the same course as His saying: «وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما لاعِبِينَ ما خَلَقْناهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ» — *“We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them for play. We did not create them except with the truth”* (44:38-39), and His saying: «وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماءَ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما باطِلاً ذلِكَ ظَنُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا» — *“We did not create the heaven and the earth and whatever is between them in vain. That is the opinion of those who are faithless”* (38:27), and other verses dealing with the proof of the Return (المعاد). The only difference is that those verses prove it through the truthfulness of His act, while the verse under discussion proves it through His being the Reality in His essence, which necessitates the truthfulness of His act.

Then, since it was possible to imagine the impossibility of reviving the dead—in which case the proof would be of no use—He dismissed this by His saying: «وَأَنَّهُ يُحْيِ الْمَوْتى» — *“and because He revives the dead.”* His reviving the dead by making dead dust a living human and making dead earth living vegetation is a continuous, witnessed reality; thus, there is no doubt in its possibility. This sentence also follows the course of His saying: «قالَ مَنْ يُحْيِ الْعِظامَ وَهِيَ رَمِيمٌ قُلْ يُحْيِيهَا الَّذِي أَنْشَأَها أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ» — *“He says, ‘Who will revive the bones when they have decayed?’ Say, ‘He will revive them who produced them the first time’”* (36:78-79), and other verses establishing the possibility of Resurrection and a second revival by way of the proof of its like occurring the first time.

Then, since it was possible to imagine that the permissibility of a second revival does not necessitate its occurrence through the attachment of power to it—deeming it far-fetched or difficult—He dismissed this by His saying: «وَأَنَّهُ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ» — *“and because He has power over all things.”* Since power is infinite, its relation to the first and second revival, and to what is easy in itself or difficult, is exactly the same; it is not mixed with incapacity, nor does it suffer from fatigue or exhaustion.

This sentence also follows the course of His saying: «أَفَعَيِينا بِالْخَلْقِ الْأَوَّلِ» — *“Were We exhausted by the first creation?”* (50:15), and His saying: «إِنَّ الَّذِي أَحْياها لَمُحْيِ الْمَوْتى إِنَّهُ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ» — *“Indeed He who revives it will revive the dead. Indeed He has power over all things”* (41:39), and other verses establishing the Resurrection through the universality and infinity of power.

These three results—meaning what is in His saying: «ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللهَ» until the end of the verse—are derived from the verse preceding them, all directed toward a single purpose: mentioning that by which the Resurrection is proven, which is what the final verse contains: «وَأَنَّ السَّاعَةَ آتِيَةٌ لا رَيْبَ فِيها وَأَنَّ اللهَ يَبْعَثُ مَنْ فِي الْقُبُورِ» — *“and that the Hour is bound to come, there is no doubt in it, and that Allah will resurrect those who are in the graves.”*

The verse only includes the resurrection of the dead and the time in which they are resurrected. As for the time, which is the Hour (الساعة), it is mentioned in His saying: «وَأَنَّ السَّاعَةَ آتِيَةٌ لا رَيْبَ فِيها» — *“and that the Hour is bound to come, there is no doubt in it.”* He did not attribute its coming to Himself by saying, for example, "and that Allah brings the Hour" or something similar. Perhaps the reason for this is the consideration that it only comes suddenly (بغتة), and no knowledge ever attaches to it, as He said: «لا تَأْتِيكُمْ إِلَّا بَغْتَةً» — *“It will not come to you but suddenly”* (7:187).

And He said: «قُلْ إِنَّما عِلْمُها عِنْدَ اللهِ» — *“Say, ‘Its knowledge is only with Allah’”* (7:187), and He said: «إِنَّ السَّاعَةَ آتِيَةٌ أَكادُ أُخْفِيها» — *“Indeed the Hour is bound to come: I almost conceal it”* (20:15). Thus, the lack of its attribution to an agent was like the lack of mentioning its time and the concealment of its arrival—an exaggeration in its hiding and a confirmation of its being a sudden surprise. It is mentioned frequently in His speech, and in none of it is an agent mentioned for it; rather, the expression was always like "coming," "it comes to them," "standing," "it stands," and the like.

As for that which is contained within the time—the revival of the dead among humans—it is what is mentioned in His saying: «وَأَنَّ اللهَ يَبْعَثُ مَنْ فِي الْقُبُورِ» — *“and that Allah will resurrect those who are in the graves.”*

If you say: The mentioned argument yields the Resurrection for all things, not just for man, because an act without a goal is vain and futile, whether it concerns man or others, yet the verse restricts itself to man only.

I say: The verse's restriction of the result to man only does not negate the proof of a similar ruling for others. However, what is needed in this context is the resurrection of man. Furthermore, it can be said that negating the Return for things other than man does not necessitate that their creation was vain on His part, because they were created for the sake of man; thus, he is the goal of their creation, and the Resurrection is the goal of man's creation.

This is what reflection on the context of the three verses provides when compared to other verses dealing with the proof of the Return in all their variety. Through this, the reason for sufficing with these results—which appear from the wording to be five, but are in reality three placed in the second verse and derived from the first, and one placed in the third verse derived from the three in the second—becomes clear.

By this, the suspicion of repetition imagined from His saying: «وَأَنَّهُ يُحْيِ الْمَوْتى» and «وَأَنَّ السَّاعَةَ آتِيَةٌ» and «وَأَنَّ اللهَ يَبْعَثُ مَنْ فِي الْقُبُورِ», etc., is also repelled.

The commentators have many different views regarding the interpretation of these three verses and the formulation of their argument, which do not lead to much benefit. In all of them, they have added foreign premises to the argument of the verse, which disrupt the smoothness of the composition and the soundness of the argument. We have refrained from mentioning them; whoever wishes to examine them should refer to the lengthy commentaries.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يُجادِلُ فِي اللهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ وَلا هُدىً وَلا كِتابٍ مُنِيرٍ» — *“Among the people is he who disputes concerning Allah without any knowledge or guidance, or an illuminating scripture”* (22:8). This is another category of people who turn away from the truth. It is said in *Kashf al-Kashshaf*, as reported: "The most apparent in the composition and the most fitting for the context is that this verse is about the followed leaders (المقلدين - with a *fathah* on the *lam*), while the previous verse—«وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يُجادِلُ» until «مَرِيدٍ»—is about the blind followers (المقلدين - with a *kasrah* on the *lam*)." End quote.

And it is indeed so, as evidenced by His saying here at the end: «لِيُضِلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ» — *“to lead [people] astray from the way of Allah”*—and His saying there: «وَيَتَّبِعُ كُلَّ شَيْطانٍ مَرِيدٍ» — *“and follows every rebellious Satan.”* Leading astray is the characteristic of the followed leader, while following is the characteristic of the blind follower.

The variation in the verse between knowledge (العلم), guidance (الهدى), and the scripture (الكتاب)—despite each of knowledge and guidance potentially encompassing the others—is evidence that what is meant by knowledge is a specific type, and by guidance a specific type. It has been said: what is meant by knowledge is necessary knowledge (العلم الضروري); by guidance is correct inference and reasoning that leads to gnosis; and by the illuminating scripture is the heavenly revelation that manifests the truth.

However, restricting "knowledge" to the "necessary" (i.e., the self-evident) has no evidence. Furthermore, "disputation" (الجدال)—whether it means absolute persistence in inquiry or the technical "dialectic" which is a syllogism composed of widely accepted and conceded premises—is a method of inference, and there is no inference regarding the self-evident at all.

It is possible that what is meant by knowledge is what is provided by rational proof (الحجة العقلية); by guidance is what is bestowed by Divine Guidance (الهداية الإلهية) upon one who is sincere to Allah in his worship and servitude, such that his heart is illuminated by the light of His gnosis (or vice versa in some way); and by the illuminating scripture is the Divine Revelation through the path of Prophethood. These are three paths to absolute knowledge: the intellect, internal vision, and hearing. The Exalted referred to them in His saying: «وَلا تَقْفُ ما لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ إِنَّ السَّمْعَ وَالْبَصَرَ وَالْفُؤادَ كُلُّ أُولئِكَ كانَ عَنْهُ مَسْؤُلاً» — *“Do not follow that of which you have no knowledge. Indeed the hearing, the eyesight, and the heart—all of these are accountable”* (17:36). And Allah knows best.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «ثانِيَ عِطْفِهِ لِيُضِلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ» — *“turning his side to lead [people] astray from the way of Allah”* (22:9) until the end of the verse. **Turning (الثني)** is bending, and the **side (العطف)** is the flank. "Turning the side" is an allusion to turning away (الإعراض), as if the one turning away bends one of his sides over the other.

His saying: «لِيُضِلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ» — *“to lead [people] astray from the way of Allah”*—is connected to His saying: «يُجادِلُ» — *“disputes.”* The "so that" (اللام) is for causation; i.e., he disputes concerning Allah out of ignorance, manifesting aversion and arrogance, in order to reach the goal of misguiding people. These are the followed leaders among the polytheists.

His saying: «لَهُ فِي الدُّنْيا خِزْيٌ وَنُذِيقُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ عَذابَ الْحَرِيقِ» — *“For him there is disgrace in this world, and on the Day of Resurrection We shall make him taste the punishment of the burning”*—is a threat of disgrace (الخزي)—which is humiliation, abasement, and scandal—in this world. This was the fate of the chieftains of the Quraysh and the great ones among the polytheists of Mecca. It is also a promise of punishment in the Hereafter.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «ذلِكَ بِما قَدَّمَتْ يَداكَ وَأَنَّ اللهَ لَيْسَ بِظَلَّامٍ لِلْعَبِيدِ» — *“‘That is because of what your hands have sent ahead, and because Allah is not tyrannical to [His] servants’”* (22:10). "That" refers to what preceded in the previous verse of the promise of disgrace and punishment. The "because of" (الباء) in «بِما قَدَّمَتْ» is for recompense, as in "I sold this for this," or for causality; i.e., the disgrace and punishment you witness is the recompense for what your hands sent ahead, or because of what your hands sent ahead of disputing concerning Allah without knowledge, guidance, or a scripture, while turning away arrogantly to misguide people. In the speech, there is a shift (التفات) from the third person to the second person to register blame and reproach.

His saying: «وَأَنَّ اللهَ لَيْسَ بِظَلَّامٍ لِلْعَبِيدِ» — *“and because Allah is not tyrannical to [His] servants”*—is coordinated with «بِما قَدَّمَتْ»; i.e., that is because Allah does not wrong His servants, but rather treats each of them according to what he deserves by his action and gives him what he asks for by the language of his state.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَعْبُدُ اللهَ عَلى حَرْفٍ» — *“And among the people is he who worships Allah on the verge...”* (22:11) until the end of the verse. The **verge (الحرف)**, the edge, and the side are synonymous. **Reassurance (الاطمئنان)** is stability and stillness. **Trial (الفتنة)**—as has been said—is ordeal. **Turning back (الانقلاب)** is returning.

This is another category of people, other than the righteous believers. It is the one who worships Allah, the Glorified, basing his worship on one side only, not all sides, and upon one decree of Allah, not every decree—and that is the side of good. The necessity of this is using religion for the sake of the world. If good befalls him, he becomes stable in the worship of Allah because of that good and is reassured by it. But if a trial or ordeal befalls him, he turns back and returns on his face without looking right or left, apostatizing from his religion out of a bad omen regarding religion or in hope of escaping the ordeal and destruction thereby. This was their habit in worshipping idols; they would worship them to attain good or escape evil through their intercession in this world, but as for the Hereafter, they did not believe in it. This wavering person who turns on his face has lost this world by falling into ordeal and destruction, and has lost the Hereafter by turning away from religion on his face, apostatizing and disbelieving. That is the manifest loss.

This is what reflection on the meaning of the verse provides. Accordingly, His saying: «يَعْبُدُ اللهَ عَلى حَرْفٍ» — *“worships Allah on the verge”*—is a type of metaphorical allusion (استعارة بالكناية). His saying: «فإن أصابه خير» — *“if good befalls him”*—etc., is an explanation and detail of His saying: "worships Allah on the verge." His saying: «خَسِرَ الدُّنْيا» — *“he loses this world”*—means by the occurrence of the trial, and «وَالْآخِرَةَ» — *“and the Hereafter”*—means by his turning on his face.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «يَدْعُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ ما لا يَضُرُّهُ وَما لا يَنْفَعُهُ ذلِكَ هُوَ الضَّلالُ الْبَعِيدُ» — *“He invokes besides Allah that which can neither harm him nor benefit him. That is extreme error”* (22:12). The one invoked is the idol; due to its lack of consciousness and will, no benefit or harm is directed from it toward its worshipper. The harm and loss that befall its worshipper only reach him from the direction of the worship itself, which is an act of his attributed to him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «يَدْعُوا لَمَنْ ضَرُّهُ أَقْرَبُ مِنْ نَفْعِهِ لَبِئْسَ الْمَوْلى وَلَبِئْسَ الْعَشِيرُ» — *“He invokes him whose harm is nearer than his benefit. An evil guardian indeed, and an evil associate!”* (22:13). The **guardian (المولى)** is the helper, and the **associate (العشير)** is the companion.

Regarding the grammatical structure of the verse's sentences, they mentioned that «يَدْعُوا» — *“he invokes”*—means "he says," and His saying: «لَمَنْ ضَرُّهُ أَقْرَبُ مِنْ نَفْعِهِ» — *“him whose harm is nearer than his benefit”*—etc., is the quoted speech. The «لَمَنْ» is a subject (مبتدأ) preceded by the "lam" of inception, and it is a relative pronoun whose relative clause is «ضَرُّهُ أَقْرَبُ مِنْ نَفْعِهِ». His saying: «لَبِئْسَ الْمَوْلى وَلَبِئْسَ الْعَشِيرُ» — *“An evil guardian indeed, and an evil associate!”*—is the response to an omitted oath, standing in place of the predicate and indicating it.

The meaning is: This person who worships idols says on the Day of Resurrection, describing his idol which he took as a guardian and associate: "The idol whose harm is nearer than its benefit is an evil guardian and an evil associate; I swear, an evil guardian indeed and an evil associate!"

Its harm is considered nearer than its benefit because he witnesses on the Day of Resurrection the eternal punishment and everlasting destruction that his worship of it entails.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «إِنَّ اللهَ يُدْخِلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ» — *“Indeed Allah will admit those who have faith and do righteous deeds into gardens with streams running in them...”* (22:14) etc. When He mentioned the three categories of disbelievers—the followed leaders who dispute concerning Allah without knowledge, the blind followers who follow every rebellious Satan and dispute like their leaders, and the wavering ones who worship Allah on the verge—and described them with misguidance and loss, He contrasted them with this category of people: those who believe and do righteous deeds. He described them with a noble dwelling and a good return, and that Allah intends this for them.

The mention of these categories serves as a preamble to what will be mentioned of the judgment between them and the detailed exposition of their state.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «مَنْ كانَ يَظُنُّ أَنْ لَنْ يَنْصُرَهُ اللهُ فِي الدُّنْيا وَالْآخِرَةِ فَلْيَمْدُدْ بِسَبِبٍ إِلَى السَّماءِ ثُمَّ لْيَقْطَعْ فَلْيَنْظُرْ هَلْ يُذْهِبَنَّ كَيْدُهُ ما يَغِيظُ» — *“Whoever thinks that Allah will not help him in this world and the Hereafter, let him stretch a rope to the ceiling and then let him hang himself, and see if his device will remove that which enrages him”* (22:15). It is said in *al-Majma‘*: "The **rope (السبب)** is everything by which a thing is reached; from this, a cord is called a *sabab*, a path is called a *sabab*, and a door is called a *sabab*." End quote. What is meant by *sabab* in the verse is the rope. **Cutting (القطع)** is well-known, and among its meanings is strangulation; it is said: "he cut," meaning he strangled himself, as if taken from "cutting off the breath."

They said: The pronoun in «لَنْ يَنْصُرَهُ اللهُ» — *“Allah will not help him”*—refers to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). This is because the polytheists of Mecca used to think that the religion brought by the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was a false novelty not built on a deep-rooted foundation, so his mention would not be raised, his religion would not spread, and he had no status with Allah. When he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) migrated to Medina and Allah, the Glorified, helped him, spreading his religion and raising his mention, that enraged them severely. So Allah, the Glorified, rebuked them with this verse, indicating that Allah is his helper and their rage will not depart; even if they were to strangle themselves, their device would have no effect.

The meaning is: Whoever among the polytheists thinks that Allah will not help His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in this world by raising his mention and spreading his religion, and in the Hereafter by forgiveness and mercy for him and the believers in him, and is then enraged by what he witnesses today of Allah's help to him—let him stretch a rope to the "sky" (meaning the ceiling, like tying the end of the rope to a high beam or the like)—then let him strangle himself with it, and see if his device and ruse will remove that which enrages him.

This is a good meaning supported by the context of the previous verses and what we previously inferred regarding the surah's revelation shortly after the Migration, while the polytheists of Mecca still possessed their power and strength.

Some mentioned: The pronoun in «لَنْ يَنْصُرَهُ» refers to the "whoever" (مَنْ), and the meaning of "cutting" is traversing the distance. The meaning of stretching a rope to the sky is ascending to it to nullify Allah's decree. The meaning would be: whoever thinks that Allah will not help him in this world and the Hereafter, let him ascend to the sky by a rope he stretches, then let him traverse the distance and see if his device will remove the decree that enrages him.

Perhaps these people mean that the intended sense of the verse is that it is obligatory for man to hope in his Lord in his worldly life and his Hereafter; if he does not hope in Him and thinks that Allah will not help him in them, and that enrages him, then let him plot whatever he plots, for it will not benefit him.

Others mentioned: The pronoun refers to the relative pronoun as in the previous view, but the meaning of "help" (النصر) is "provision" (الرزق), as it is said: "a helped land" (أرض منصورة), meaning one rained upon. The meaning would be as in the first view.

This is closer to consideration than the one before it and better, but both views are subject to the objection that they necessitate the verse's disconnection from the verses preceding it. Furthermore, it would be more appropriate according to these two views to say: "whoever thinks (من ظن)..." etc., not "whoever used to think (من كان يظن)," which suggests the continuation of the thought from him in the past; this supports the first view.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَكَذلِكَ أَنْزَلْناهُ آياتٍ بَيِّناتٍ وَأَنَّ اللهَ يَهْدِي مَنْ يُرِيدُ» — *“Thus have We sent it down as manifest signs, and Allah guides whomever He wishes”* (22:16). It has been mentioned repeatedly that this is a case of likening the universal to its particular instance by claiming distinctness, to indicate that the ruling found in the particular instance applies to the rest of its instances. It is like one who points to Zayd and ‘Amr while they are speaking and walking on their feet and says: "Thus is man," meaning the ruling of speaking and walking on feet applies to all individuals. So the meaning of His saying: «وَكَذلِكَ أَنْزَلْناهُ آياتٍ بَيِّناتٍ» is: We sent down the Quran, and it consists of verses with clear indications, just as in the previous verses of this surah.

His saying: «وَأَنَّ اللهَ يَهْدِي مَنْ يُرِيدُ» — *“and Allah guides whomever He wishes”*—is the predicate of an omitted subject; i.e., "and the matter is that Allah guides whomever He wishes." As for the one He does not wish to guide, there is no guide for him. Thus, the mere fact that the verses are manifest is not sufficient for the guidance of one who hears them or reflects upon them, unless Allah wishes his guidance.

It is also said: The sentence is coordinated with the pronoun in «أَنْزَلْناهُ»; the implied meaning is: "and thus We sent down that Allah guides whomever He wishes." The first view is clearer in its connection to the beginning of the verse, and it is evident.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: regarding the saying of the Exalted: «وَيَتَّبِعُ كُلَّ شَيْطانٍ مَرِيدٍ» — he said: "The rebellious (المريد) is the wicked one."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi Hatim reported from Abu Zayd: regarding His saying: «وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يُجادِ لُ فِي اللهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ» — he said: "It was revealed concerning al-Nadr ibn al-Harith."

**I say:** It was also narrated by Ibn Jarir and Ibn al-Mundhir from Ibn Jurayj. It appears to be a case of application (تطبيق), as is their habit in most narrations dealing with the occasions of revelation (شأن النزول). Accordingly, the view that the subsequent verse—«وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يُجادِلُ فِي اللهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ وَلا هُدىً»—was revealed concerning him, as reported from Mujahid, is more appropriate than the view that this verse was revealed concerning him. This is because the man was among the prominent figures of the people, and this verse, as mentioned, is about the followers, while the other verse is about the followed leaders.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: regarding the saying of the Exalted: «مُخَلَّقَةٍ وَغَيْرِ مُخَلَّقَةٍ» — he said: "The formed (المخلقة) is when it becomes complete, and the unformed (غير مخلقة) is the miscarriage."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ahmad, al-Bukhari, Muslim, Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Majah, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Bayhaqi in *Shu‘ab al-Iman* reported from ‘Abdullah ibn Mas‘ud, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—and he is the truthful and the verified—narrated to us: **إن أحدكم يجمع خلقه في بطن أمه أربعين يوما نطفة ـ ثم يكون علقة مثل ذلك ثم يكون مضغة مثل ذلك ـ ثم يرسل إليه الملك فينفخ فيه الروح ويؤمر بأربع كلمات ـ يكتب رزقه وأجله وعمله وشقي أو سعيد. فو الذي لا إله غيره إن أحدكم ليعمل بعمل أهل الجنة ـ حتى ما يكون بينه وبينها إلا ذراع فيسبق عليه الكتاب ـ فيعمل بعمل أهل النار فيدخلها ـ وإن أحدكم ليعمل بعمل أهل النار حتى ما يكون بينه وبينها إلا ذراع ـ فيسبق عليه الكتاب فيعمل بعمل أهل الجنة ـ فيدخلها.** — 'Indeed, each of you is gathered in his creation in his mother's womb for forty days as a drop, then he becomes a clot for a similar period, then he becomes a lump for a similar period. Then the angel is sent to him and breathes the spirit into him, and is commanded with four words: to write his provision, his lifespan, his deeds, and whether he is wretched or felicitous. By the One besides whom there is no god, indeed one of you may perform the deeds of the people of Paradise until there is but an arm's length between him and it, then the Book overtakes him, and he performs the deeds of the people of the Fire and enters it. And indeed one of you may perform the deeds of the people of the Fire until there is but an arm's length between him and it, then the Book overtakes him, and he performs the deeds of the people of Paradise and enters it.'"

**I say:** The narration is reported through other paths from him, and from Ibn ‘Abbas, Anas, and Hudhayfah ibn Asid. There are some differences in their texts. In some of them—which is what Ibn Jarir narrated from Ibn Mas‘ud: **يقال للملك : انطلق إلى أم الكتاب فاستنسخ منه صفة هذه النطفة ـ فينطلق فينسخها فلا يزال معه ـ حتى يأتي على آخر صفتها** — "It is said to the angel: 'Go to the Mother of the Book (أم الكتاب) and transcribe from it the description of this drop.' So he goes and transcribes it, and it remains with him until he reaches the end of its description." (The hadith).

Similar reports have been narrated through Shi‘i paths from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), as in *Qurb al-Isnad* by al-Himyari from Ahmad ibn Muhammad from Ahmad ibn Abi Nasr from Imam al-Rida (عليه السلام), which states: **فإذا تمت الأربعة الأشهر بعث الله تبارك وتعالى إليها ملكين خلاقين ـ يصورانه ويكتبان رزقه وأجله وشقيا أو سعيدا.** — "When the four months are completed, Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, sends to it two creating angels who fashion it and write its provision, its lifespan, and whether it is wretched or felicitous." (The hadith).

We have already presented in the commentary of the beginning of Surah Al ‘Imran the hadith of *al-Kafi* from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام) regarding the fashioning of the embryo and the writing of what is decreed for it, which states: **أن الملكين يكتبان جميع ما قدر له عن لوح ـ يقرع جبهة أمه فيكتبان جميع ما في اللوح ـ ويشترطان البداء فيما يكتبان** — "The two angels write everything decreed for it from a tablet that strikes the forehead of its mother; they write everything in the tablet and make the condition of *al-Bada’* (البداء) — *the possibility of change in the non-inevitable divine decree* — in what they write." (The hadith). There are others with the same meaning.

The implication of this hadith and those like it is the permissibility of change in what was written for the child, while the implication of what preceded is the opposite. However, there is no contradiction between the two meanings, for everything—including man—has a portion in the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ) in which there is no way for change or substitution in what is written, and a portion in the Tablet of Effacement and Confirmation (لوح المحو والإثبات) which accepts change and substitution. Thus, the decree is of two types: inevitable (محتوم) and non-inevitable (غير محتوم). The Exalted said: «يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ وَعِنْدَهُ أُمُّ الْكِتابِ» — *“Allah effaces or confirms whatever He wishes, and with Him is the Mother of the Book”* (13:39).

The discussion regarding the meaning of the Decree (القضاء) has already preceded, and it became clear through it that the tablet of the Decree, whatever it may be, corresponds to the system of cause and effect (العلية والمعلولية) and is divided into two series: the series of complete causes (العلل التامة) and their effects, which do not accept change; and the series of incomplete causes (العلل الناقصة) with their effects, which are susceptible to it. It is as if the first category of narrations refers to the inevitable decree for the embryo, and the second to the other. We have also clarified previously that the inevitability of the decree for the servant's act does not negate the voluntary nature (اختيارية) of the act; so remember this.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission from Sallam ibn al-Mustanir, who said: "I asked Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام) about the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: «مُخَلَّقَةٍ وَغَيْرِ مُخَلَّقَةٍ» — He said: **المخلقة هم الذر الذين خلقهم الله في صلب آدم صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله ، أخذ عليهم الميثاق ثم أجراهم في أصلاب الرجال وأرحام النساء ـ وهم الذين يخرجون إلى الدنيا حتى يسألوا عن الميثاق. وأما قوله : «وَغَيْرِ مُخَلَّقَةٍ» ـ فهم كل نسمة لم يخلقهم الله عز وجل في صلب آدم حين خلق الذر ـ وأخذ عليهم الميثاق ، وهم النطف من العزل والسقط ـ قبل أن ينفخ فيه الروح والحياة والبقاء.** — 'The formed (المخلقة) are the particles (الذر) whom Allah created in the loin of Adam (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم); He took the covenant from them, then ran them through the loins of men and the wombs of women. They are those who come out into the world until they are asked about the covenant. As for His saying: "and the unformed" (وَغَيْرِ مُخَلَّقَةٍ), they are every soul whom Allah, Mighty and Majestic, did not create in the loin of Adam when He created the particles and took the covenant from them; they are the drops from coitus interruptus and miscarriages before the spirit, life, and permanence are breathed into them.'"

**I say:** The clarification of the hadith's meaning has already preceded in the traditionary discourse related to the Verse of the Covenant (آية الذر) in Surah al-A‘raf.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, with its chain of transmission from ‘Ali ibn al-Mughirah from Abu ‘Abdillah from his father (عليه السلام), who said: **إذا بلغ العبد مائة سنة فذلك أرذل العمر.** — "When the servant reaches a hundred years, that is the most abject age."

**I say:** Some narrations with this meaning have already preceded in the commentary of Surah al-Nahl under verse 70.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi Hatim and Ibn Mardawayh reported with a sound chain from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: "Some people from the Bedouins used to come to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and submit to Islam. When they returned to their lands, if they found a year of rain, a year of fertility, and a year of good births, they would say: 'This religion of ours is righteous, so hold fast to it.' But if they found a year of drought, a year of bad births, and a year of famine, they would say: 'There is no good in this religion of ours.' So Allah revealed: «وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَعْبُدُ اللهَ عَلى حَرْفٍ»."

**I say:** This meaning is also narrated from him through other paths.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission from Zurarah from Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام), who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: «وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَعْبُدُ اللهَ عَلى حَرْفٍ» — He said: **نعم قوم وحدوا الله وخلعوا عبادة من يعبد من دون الله ـ فخرجوا من الشرك ولم يعرفوا أن محمدا صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله رسول الله ـ فهم يعبدون الله على شك في محمد وما جاء به ـ فأتوا رسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وقالوا : ننظر فإن كثرت أموالنا وعوفينا في أنفسنا وأولادنا علمنا ـ أنه صادق وأنه رسول الله : وإن كان غير ذلك نظرنا. قال الله عز وجل : «فَإِنْ أَصابَهُ خَيْرٌ اطْمَأَنَّ بِهِ» ـ يعني عافية في الدنيا ـ «وَإِنْ أَصابَتْهُ فِتْنَةٌ» يعني بلاء في نفسه ـ «انْقَلَبَ عَلى وَجْهِهِ» انقلب على شكه إلى الشرك ـ «خَسِرَ الدُّنْيا وَالْآخِرَةَ ذلِكَ هُوَ الْخُسْرانُ الْمُبِينُ ـ يَدْعُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ ما لا يَضُرُّهُ وَما لا يَنْفَعُهُ» ـ قال : ينقلب مشركا يدعو غير الله ويعبد غيره.** — 'Yes, they are a people who declared the oneness of Allah and cast off the worship of those worshipped besides Allah; they emerged from polytheism but did not recognize that Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is the Messenger of Allah. So they worship Allah while in doubt concerning Muhammad and what he brought. They came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: "We shall see; if our wealth increases and we are granted well-being in ourselves and our children, we will know that he is truthful and that he is the Messenger of Allah; but if it is otherwise, we shall see." Allah, Mighty and Majestic, said: "if good befalls him, he is reassured by it"—meaning well-being in the world—"but if a trial befalls him"—meaning an affliction in himself—"he turns back on his face"—he turns back upon his doubt to polytheism—"he loses this world and the Hereafter. That is the manifest loss. He invokes besides Allah that which can neither harm him nor benefit him"—He said: he turns back as a polytheist, invoking other than Allah and worshipping other than Him.'" (The hadith).

I say: Al-Saduq narrated it in *al-Tawhid* with a slight variation.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Faryabi, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), and Ibn Mardawayh reported from Ibn ‘Abbas: regarding His saying: «مَنْ كانَ يَظُنُّ أَنْ لَنْ يَنْصُرَهُ اللهُ» — he said: **من كان يظن أن لن ينصر الله محمدا في الدنيا والآخرة ـ «فَلْيَمْدُدْ بِسَبِبٍ» قال : فليربط حبلا ـ «إِلَى السَّماءِ» قال : إلى سماء بيته السقف ـ «ثُمَّ لْيَقْطَعْ» قال ثم يختنق به حتى يموت.** — "Whoever thinks that Allah will not help Muhammad in this world and the Hereafter—'let him stretch a rope'—he said: let him tie a cord—'to the sky'—he said: to the 'sky' of his house, the ceiling—'then let him cut'—he said: then let him strangle himself with it until he dies."

I say: Although this is an interpretation from him, it carries the meaning of the occasion of revelation, which is why we have cited it.
